title: Bank Statement Analysis for E-Commerce Businesses
meta: E-commerce businesses have unique revenue patterns including platform payouts, returns, and ad spend. Learn how lenders analyze bank statements for online retail businesses.
content:
E-Commerce Revenue Is Different

E-commerce businesses receive revenue through platform payouts — Shopify Payments, Stripe, PayPal, Amazon Seller Central, or Square — rather than direct customer payments. These appear as periodic transfers (daily, weekly, or bi-weekly) from the payment platform to the business bank account. Lenders must recognize these payout deposits as genuine business revenue.

Platform Payout Patterns

Each platform has a different payout schedule: Shopify Payments pays daily or every 2 days; Stripe pays every 2 business days; Amazon pays every 14 days; PayPal allows manual transfers. This means an e-commerce business may show irregular deposit timing even with consistent underlying sales — a lender unfamiliar with e-commerce might incorrectly flag this as inconsistent income.

Returns and Chargebacks

E-commerce businesses experience returns and chargebacks — deductions that appear as negative adjustments or reversed deposits. Lenders should net these against gross deposits to arrive at true net revenue rather than using gross payouts as income.

High Ad Spend Expenses

Successful e-commerce businesses often spend 15–30% of revenue on advertising (Google Ads, Meta Ads, TikTok Ads). These appear as recurring charges from advertising platforms and significantly increase the expense ratio. Lenders should use an industry-appropriate expense ratio of 50–70% for e-commerce.

Seasonality in E-Commerce

E-commerce revenue is heavily seasonal — Q4 (October through December) typically represents 30–40% of annual revenue for many categories. Analyzing only Q4 statements would dramatically overstate qualifying income. Twelve months of statements are required for accurate income calculation.

Inventory Financing Confusion

Growing e-commerce businesses often use inventory financing or lines of credit that appear as deposits. These are loans, not revenue, and must be excluded from income calculations. Lenders should flag large deposits that correspond to round numbers and verify their source.

StatementScrub identifies platform payout patterns, nets returns against gross revenue, and applies appropriate e-commerce expense ratios for accurate income analysis.
